perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Page 5 of 6 © 2019 Fintricity Group – Private and Confidential NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT Page 6 of 6 © 2019 Fintricity Group – Private and Confidential England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of t he English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts. Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement must be commenced within one (1) year after the cause of action accrues.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a one-year limitation period for legal actions may conflict with statutory limitation periods in England for breach of contract claims, which are typically longer than one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Limitation Act 1980, section 5, generally provides a six-year limitation period for actions founded on simple contract. By stipulating a one-year limitation period, the modified text directly contradicts this statutory provision, potentially rendering the contractual limitation unenforceable under English law.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a one-year limitation period for legal actions, which contradicts the standard six-year limitation period for contract claims under English law, as indicated by the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet refers to the Limitation Act 1980, implying a legal framework governing limitation periods, and although the snippet itself does not explicitly state the six-year period, the LLM Law Explanation refers to section 5 which does state that. By shortening the limitation period, the modified text creates an inconsistency with established legal standards, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable and constituting a legal contradiction as it violates the law.

perturbation.original_text: 13 The Company agrees that, during the term of this agreement and for a period of 48 months thereafter, the Company shall not, whether by itself, its officers, personnel, contractors or agents of the Company or whether by a Company A f f i l i a t e o r t h e o f f i c e r s , p e r s o n n e l , c o n t r a c t o r s o r a g e n t s o f that Company Affiliates, solicit or seek to solicit orders from any customer of Fintricity for services similar to the services which Fintricity provides to t hat customer without the prior written consent of Fintricity.
perturbation.changed_text: 13 The Company agrees that, during the term of this agreement and for a period of 120 months thereafter, the Company shall not, whether by itself, its officers, personnel, contractors or agents of the Company or whether by a Company A f f i l i a t e o r t h e o f f i c e r s , p e r s o n n e l , c o n t r a c t o r s o r a g e n t s o f that Company Affiliates, solicit or seek to solicit orders from any customer of Fintricity for services similar to the services which Fintricity provides to t hat customer without the prior written consent of Fintricity.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 120 months (10 years) may be deemed an unreasonably long restraint of trade, especially in jurisdictions with laws against overly broad non-compete clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes an exception, a 10-year restriction is likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it prevents the former party from soliciting any customer, regardless of the nature or length of their relationship with Fintricity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from 48 months to 120 months. The Law Explanation states that under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, a 10-year restriction is likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 16600(a), states that every contract restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is void to that extent. The increased non-solicitation period contradicts this law as it imposes a longer restriction, potentially hindering the company's ability to conduct business.

perturbation.original_text: 9 Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, eac h recipient shall promptly deliver up to the disclosing party all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and destroy or erase any Confidential Informatio n contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device.
perturbation.changed_text: 9 Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, each recipient may retain indefinitely all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and is not obligated to destroy or erase any Confidential Information contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the recipient to indefinitely retain confidential information after termination of the agreement contradicts the general principle that confidential information should be returned or destroyed upon termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: While the DTSA doesn't mandate the return or destruction of confidential information, it protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. Retaining confidential information indefinitely after termination of the agreement increases the risk of misappropriation, which could lead to a violation of the DTSA if the information is used in a way that harms the disclosing party. The modification removes an important safeguard against future misappropriation claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of protecting trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Snippet 1 (18 U.S. Code § 1836) focuses on civil proceedings related to misappropriation of trade secrets, the change from requiring destruction of confidential information to allowing its indefinite retention significantly increases the risk of future misappropriation. This weakens the original contract's safeguard against potential violations of trade secret law. Snippet 2 is unavailable.

perturbation.original_text: 11 It is agreed that any overt or covert action of circumvention p rescribed by this agreement shall constitute a fraudulent act against the other p arty and will be subject to judicial action, recompense for damages, possible pu nitive damages and injunctive relieve as imposed by the legal process.
perturbation.changed_text: 11 It is agreed that any overt or covert action of circumvention prescribed by this agreement shall constitute a fraudulent act against the other party and will be subject to judicial action, recompense for damages capped at $100, and injunctive relief as imposed by the legal process.
perturbation.explanation: Capping damages for fraud at $100 is likely unenforceable and contrary to public policy, as it shields the breaching party from meaningful liability for fraudulent conduct. Fraudulent acts typically allow for substantial damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Fraudulent Misrepresentation
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law, as reflected in the Restatement (Second) of Torts § 549, allows for the recovery of actual damages and, in many jurisdictions, punitive damages for fraudulent misrepresentation. Capping damages at $100 is a substantial deviation from these principles and likely unenforceable as unconscionable or against public policy. Setting a $100 cap effectively nullifies the legal remedies available for fraud.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: fraud | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you fraud Fraud is both a civil tort and criminal wrong. In civil litigation , allegations of fraud might be based on a misrepresentation of fact that was either intentional or negligent . For a statement to be an intentional misrepresentation, the person who made it must either have known the statement was false or been reckless as to its truth. The speaker must have also intended that the person to whom the statement was made would rely on it. The hearer must then have reasonably relied on the promise and also been harmed because of that reliance . A claim for fraud based on a negligent misrepresentation differs in that the speaker of the false statement may have actually believed it to be true; however, the speaker lacked reasonable grounds for that belief. A promise that goes unfulfilled may give rise to a claim for fraud only under particular circumstances. For example, in California law, a false promise is only fraudulent if the promisor intended both not to perform on the promise and also that that the promisee would rely on the promise; and, the promisee must have reasonably relied on the promise and been harmed as a result of that reasonable reliance . When the promise was made as part of a contract , most states forbid a plaintiff from recovering under both contract law and tort law. Lastly, opinions are not usually actionable as fraud except under very specific circumstances defined by either the common law or statutes in each state. In California, for example , a jury may be instructed that an opinion can be considered a representation of fact if it is proven that the speaker “claimed to have special knowledge of the subject matter” that the listener did not have; OR that the representation was made “not in a casual expression of belief, but in a way that declared the matter to be true;” OR if the speaker was in a position of “trust and confidence” over the listener; OR if the listener “had some other special reason to expect” the speaker to be reliable. Once the jury decides that an opinion qualifies as a misrepresentation of fact under the circumstances, the plaintiff must still demonstrate all of the other elements of an intentional or negligent misrepresentation already described, such as reasonable reliance and resulting harm. In criminal law, fraud usually takes very specific forms, such as bankruptcy fraud , credit card fraud , or healthcare fraud . California law, for example, also recognizes distinct crimes for check fraud , access card fraud , insurance fraud , and making false financial statements . Some criminal fraud statutes might be classified under laws forbidding larceny , others under forgery , and others as a crime covered by laws regarding a specific industry, like insurance or banking laws. Suspicions of criminal fraud should be reported to law enforcement authorities. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text caps damages for fraud at $100, which directly contradicts the principle that victims of fraud are entitled to recompense for their losses. The Law Explanation indicates that common law allows for recovery of actual damages and potentially punitive damages for fraudulent misrepresentation. The scraped law snippet from Wex states that in civil litigation, fraud allegations might be based on intentional or negligent misrepresentation, requiring the hearer to be harmed because of that reliance. Capping damages at $100 effectively nullifies the potential for recompense, creating a direct contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also confirms this by stating that such a cap shields the breaching party from meaningful liability, which is against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: 14 Neither party shall make or permit others to make any reference to the subject matter of the Agreement, or the Confidential Information or use the name of the other party in any public announ cements, promotional, marketing o r s a l e s materials or efforts without the prior written consent of the o ther party and such consent shall not be unrea sonably withheld or delayed.
perturbation.changed_text: 14 Neither party shall make or permit others to make any reference to the subject matter of the Agreement, or the Confidential Information or use the name of the other party in any public announcements, promotional, marketing or sales materials or efforts. Consent to use the other party's name or the subject matter of this agreement can be withheld for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'unreasonably withheld or delayed' clause gives one party absolute discretion to withhold consent, which is against the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract within its scope. While an NDA is not directly within the UCC's scope, many jurisdictions apply the principle of good faith and fair dealing to all contracts. Allowing one party to withhold consent for any reason undermines the implied duty of good faith and can be deemed an unenforceable term.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The changed text allows consent to be withheld 'for any reason,' directly contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' While the UCC might not directly apply, the principle of good faith is widely applicable. By allowing consent to be withheld for any reason, the changed text creates a scenario where a party could act in bad faith, undermining the agreement's purpose.
